title: Retrieval-Grounded Evaluation
concept: Evaluating RAG systems requires checking whether every generated fact can be directly mapped to a specific source document chunk (grounding).

exercises.prompt: Why do we need to evaluate if an AI's an AI's answer is grounded?

options.text: To make sure the AI only uses facts from our source documents.
To make the AI write creative fictional stories.
To test how fast the AI can type.

exercises.explain: Retrieval-Grounded Evaluation focuses on ensuring the AI's response is directly anchored to the source documents we gave it.

cards.title: Keep the AI Honest
cards.body: AI systems are famous for making up facts with absolute confidence—this is called hallucination. How do we keep them honest? By forcing them to prove that every single sentence they output is grounded in a real source document!

cards.title: What is Grounding?
cards.body: Retrieval-Grounded Evaluation checks if an AI's answer is fully supported by the retrieved document chunks. If the AI states a fact that cannot be found in the provided sources, the answer is not 'grounded', even if it sounds correct.

cards.title: The Digital Receipt Analogy
cards.body: Imagine you are writing a history essay, but you must prove that every single sentence you write comes from a specific page in a history textbook. If you write 'George Washington had wooden teeth' but it's not in the textbook, you aren't grounded. Grounding is like having a digital receipt for every fact you state!

cards.title: Grounded vs. Un-grounded
cards.body:
User asks: 'When was the company founded?'
Retrieved document: 'Acme Corp started in 1998.'
AI Answer A: 'Acme Corp was founded in 1998.' (Grounded! ✅)
AI Answer B: 'Acme Corp was founded in 1998 by John Smith.' (Not fully grounded! ❌ The name John Smith wasn't in the source document.)

cards.title: The Truth Myth
cards.body: Some people think that if an AI's answer is factually true in the real world, it is grounded. Actually, even if a fact is true, it is considered UN-GROUNDED in RAG if it wasn't present in the specific retrieved documents provided to the AI.

cards.title: Grounding Comparison
cards.body: Let's compare a grounded response to an ungrounded one based on a source text.

left.text: States only facts found directly in the source document chunks. No extra guesses.

right.text: Adds external knowledge or guesses not mentioned in the source documents.

options.text: Whether the answer uses poetic and engaging language.
Whether every fact in the answer is supported by the retrieved source documents.
Whether the answer is longer than 500 words.
Whether the AI used the fastest hardware to generate the response.

exercises.explain: Grounding evaluation checks if the generated answer is strictly supported by the retrieved documents, preventing the AI from hallucinating external information.

exercises.prompt: True or False: If an AI states a true fact that is NOT in the retrieved documents, the answer is still considered grounded.
exercises.explain: Grounding strictly requires that the information comes from the retrieved documents. Even if a fact is true in reality, it is ungrounded if the source documents don't mention it.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing word to complete the rule of evaluation.
exercises.sentence: If an AI answer contains facts not found in the source documents, it is not considered ________.
exercises.explain: A response is only 'grounded' if all of its facts can be traced directly back to the provided source documents.

exercises.prompt: Source document: 'The software company was established in 2010.' Which generated answer is NOT fully grounded?

options.text: 'The software company was started in 2010 in Seattle, Washington.'

exercises.explain: Option B introduces a brand new fact ('in Seattle, Washington') that is not found anywhere in the provided source text, making it ungrounded.

exercises.prompt: Why is retrieval-grounded evaluation so important when building secure, fact-based AI systems?

options.text: It ensures every claim in the answer is backed by a specific source document chunk.
It makes the AI answer questions much faster using cached data.
It translates the user's query into multiple foreign languages automatically.
It automatically corrects spelling mistakes in the user's original query.

exercises.explain: Grounding ensures that the AI only says things that are explicitly supported by the retrieved source documents. This stops the AI from inventing facts or using outdated training data, resulting in reliable outputs.
